response:
SECTION 1  
the squirrel is sitting in front of the second hollow. it makes two statements. the first statement says there are no nuts in the other hollow. the second statement claims there is at least one nut in one of the hollows. these statements need to be evaluated to determine if the squirrel is red or gray.  

SECTION 2  
red squirrels always tell the truth, while gray squirrels always lie. the statements made by the squirrel must be analyzed for consistency. if both statements are true, the squirrel is red. if both are false, the squirrel is gray.  

SECTION 3  
the first statement says there are no nuts in the other hollow. if this is true, then the second hollow must have all the nuts. the second statement says there is at least one nut in one of the hollows. if both statements are true, the second hollow must have nuts.  

SECTION 4  
if the squirrel is red, both statements are true. this would mean the second hollow has all the nuts. if the squirrel is gray, both statements are false. this would mean the other hollow has nuts, and neither hollow has nuts, which is a contradiction.  

SECTION 5  
the second statement cannot be false if the first is true. if the first statement is true, the other hollow has no nuts. the second statement says there is at least one nut in one of the hollows. this means the second hollow must have nuts. if the squirrel is red, both statements are true. if the squirrel is gray, both statements are false, which is not possible.  

SECTION 6  
the only consistent scenario is that the squirrel is red. both statements are true, meaning the second hollow has nuts. this satisfies the condition that the squirrel is telling the truth. therefore, the squirrel is red.
